chapters.chapter_summary: The patient was positioned supine with a shoulder roll elevating the right shoulder and head turned to the left, exposing the temporal-parietal area. A red line indicated the planned incision site on the scalp.

I performed an incision in the dura mater using a sharp tool, and the surrounding area was held steady with other instruments. The dura mater was opened, revealing the brain underneath. I used various instruments to carefully separate the dura from the brain tissue.

I identified and circled the tumor in yellow. I used forceps and other tools to carefully separate the tumor from surrounding tissues. I continued to use various instruments to remove the tumor, ensuring minimal damage to the surrounding brain tissue.

I performed the resection of the tumor using computer navigation and neurophysiological monitoring with brain mapping. I observed the tumor being removed in one piece in a gross total fashion. I identified and removed additional suspicious areas for residual tumor.

I used hydrogen peroxide solution-soaked cotton balls to treat the remaining brain tissue around the craniotomy site for both destruction of neoplastic cells and hemostasis. I covered the cavity with a hemostatic agent.

I reapproximated the dura mater and performed duroplasty. I re-approximated the bone flap and covered the border between the bone flap and the cranium with synthetic bone graft material to achieve a better cosmetic result.
I observed that the patient had no neurological deficit and was discharged home. Post-operative MRI scans showed no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent tumor.

SOAP.Subjective: I performed a brain tumor resection on a 20-plus year old patient who presented with several weeks history of headache, progressive weakness on the left side of the body, as well as difficulty with gait and balance. On physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented but had some left hemiparesis with upper tract signs.
SOAP.Objective: I identified a large mass at the temporoparietal junction with significant local mass effect and vasogenic edema. I performed the resection using computer navigation and neurophysiological monitoring with brain mapping. I observed the tumor being removed in one piece in a gross total fashion. I used sodium fluorescein guided microscopy to confirm the location of the tumor and to identify any residual tumor.
SOAP.Assessment: I assessed the procedure as successful with no injury to the surrounding brain. Post-operative MRI scans showed no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. The pathology of the tumor was glioblastoma multiforme with MGMT promoter methylation and IDH1 or IDH2 mutation not detected.
SOAP.Plan: I closed the wound in layers and covered the resection cavity with hemostatic agents. I re-approximated the dura mater and performed duroplasty. I re-approximated the bone flap and covered the border with synthetic bone graft material. The patient had no neurological deficit and was discharged home.
